title: Unauthorized production change — no approved CR matches the observed change
exclusions: Does NOT evaluate the contents of an approved change; focuses on authorisation only.
evidence: Hourly search 'unauthorized_prod_change' writes findings to compliance_summary.

compliance.assurance_rationale: CC8.1 requires that changes are authorised, designed, documented, and tested; the UC enforces the authorisation gate technically.
compliance.controlObjective: Evidence that SOC 2 CC8.1 (Change management) is enforced — Splunk UC-22.42.1: Unauthorized production change — no approved CR matches the observed change.
compliance.evidenceArtifact: Saved search 'UC-22.42.1' running on catalogue-defined data sources (see UC detailedImplementation), archived to the restricted audit_evidence index (default 7-year retention). Auto-drafted — SME review required.

compliance.assurance_rationale: CM-3 configuration-change control — the UC is a direct implementation.
compliance.controlObjective: Evidence that NIST 800-53 CM-3 is enforced — Splunk UC-22.42.1: Unauthorized production change — no approved CR matches the observed change.
compliance.evidenceArtifact: Saved search 'UC-22.42.1' running on catalogue-defined data sources (see UC detailedImplementation), archived to the restricted audit_evidence index (default 7-year retention). Auto-drafted — SME review required.

compliance.assurance_rationale: SOX ITGC change authorisation — the UC is a textbook control test.
compliance.controlObjective: Evidence that SOX ITGC ITGC.ChangeMgmt.Authorization (Change authorised) is enforced — Splunk UC-22.42.1: Unauthorized production change — no approved CR matches the observed change.
compliance.evidenceArtifact: Saved search 'UC-22.42.1' running on catalogue-defined data sources (see UC detailedImplementation), archived to the restricted audit_evidence index (default 7-year retention). Auto-drafted — SME review required.

controlTest.positiveScenario: When a production-system change event arrives without a matching approved CR within the 60-minute correlation window, the UC fires.
controlTest.negativeScenario: Changes with a CR in approved_state at the time of execution do NOT fire.

dataSources: See subcategory preamble. — supplemented by authentication logs, system audit trails, and configuration management database (CMDB) asset inventory exports
spl:
index=change sourcetype=prod:change earliest=-1h
| join type=outer change_id [search index=itsm sourcetype=servicenow:change earliest=-1h state=approved | fields change_id approved_at]
| where isnull(approved_at)
| table _time change_id system applied_by system_owner
description: Validates every production change event against the CR database in real time. Any change without an approved CR is an immediate ITGC deficiency — and a potential security event.
value: Replaces the standard quarterly-sample audit with continuous evidence of the authorisation gate.
implementation: (1) Force CI/CD tools to emit change events tagged with change_id; (2) ingest CR state from the ITSM platform; (3) run hourly; (4) on finding, escalate via SOAR. Indexes required: `index=change`, `index=itsm`; Sourcetypes: `sourcetype=prod:change`, `sourcetype=servicenow:change`.
visualization: Stacked bar approved vs unauthorised by system, table of findings, single-value '% authorised changes'.

references.title: MITRE ATT&CK — T1562.001 Impair Defenses: Disable Tools

knownFalsePositives:
1. **Authorized change management activities** — Approved changes processed through the formal change advisory board (CAB) produce control deviation signals during implementation windows that are expected and documented in the approved change record.

2. **Annual control testing by external auditors** — During SOC 2 Type II examination periods, auditors perform control testing activities that generate unusual access patterns and sampling events that are legitimate examination procedures.

3. **Business continuity and disaster recovery drills** — Scheduled BCP/DR exercises intentionally trigger failover events, service interruptions, and recovery procedures that produce control monitoring alerts as expected test outcomes.

4. **Vendor access for approved maintenance** — Third-party vendors granted time-limited access for approved maintenance activities (patching, upgrades, troubleshooting) generate elevated privilege usage that is covered by the vendor access management procedure.

**Suppression mechanism:** Maintain a `soc2_exception_register` KV Store lookup containing approved change record IDs, scheduled audit periods, DR drill windows, and vendor access tickets. The search joins against this lookup and suppresses matches with valid, non-expired exception entries.
detailedImplementation:
## Prerequisites

• **Splunk**: Enterprise ≥9.2 or Splunk Cloud (current). Scheduling requires a search head not under memory pressure (check `index=_internal sourcetype=splunkd component=ProcessRunner` for OOM warnings before deploying a new scheduled search).
• **Indexes**: `change`, `itsm` must exist on all indexers with retention matching the regulation's minimum (PCI: 1y, HIPAA: 6y, NIST 800-53: 1-3y per FIPS 199, SOX: 7y). Set `frozenTimePeriodInSecs` accordingly in `indexes.conf`.
• **Search-head capacity**: Subsearch-based detections need `subsearch_maxtime` ≥ 60s and `subsearch_maxout` ≥ 50000 in `limits.conf`. For high-volume environments, tune `[search] max_searches_per_cpu` up from default.
• **Regulatory mapping**: This detection evidences clause `CC8.1`. The control objective is: "Evidence that SOC 2 CC8.1 (Change management) is enforced — Splunk UC-22.42.1: Unauthorized production change — no approved CR matches the observed change." — every alert payload should include this text so downstream consumers know the compliance purpose.

## Step 1 — Configure data collection

Data collection is bespoke for this UC — no single canonical TA parses every source the detection relies on. Route data into `change`, `itsm` under `prod:change`, `servicenow:change`, and document the ingestion path in the UC runbook so future engineers know where each field originates.


Four distinct operational considerations shape this detection's rollout:


• **Hint 1:** Force CI/CD tools to emit change events tagged with change_id. CIM-compliance tagging must be validated end-to-end: a downstream datamodel that does not accelerate because of a missing tag is a silent failure.

• **Hint 2:** ingest CR state from the ITSM platform. Ingestion SLA drift is usually invisible until it breaks something — monitor the feed's freshness with a companion search that alerts when the newest event is older than the expected cadence.

• **Hint 3:** run hourly. This is an operational consideration that influences either the detection's accuracy or its maintainability — document the approach chosen in the UC runbook.

• **Hint 4:** on finding, escalate via SOAR. Automated remediation must be gated behind a defined approval workflow; compliance controls that take destructive action without human review are themselves a governance risk.

## Step 2 — Implement the search

For "Unauthorized production change — no approved CR matches the observed change," the aggregation exposes change activity — change frequency, change authorship, and change surface are the dimensions the regulation cares about, not raw events.

This search correlates `index=change` (sourcetype=`prod:change`) on the outer side with `index=itsm` (sourcetype=`servicenow:change`) on the inner side, joined on `change_id` across the last hour. `type=outer` (left-outer) returns every row from the outer search and fills nulls where the inner search has no match. Use this when the compliance question is 'for every row in A, what does B say?' — the null pattern is itself the finding (e.g. 'ticket opened but no PIR recorded').

Join caveat: Splunk's `| join` subsearch is capped at `max_searches_per_cpu * subsearch_maxout` results (default: 50000). If the inner subsearch exceeds this, rows are silently dropped and the resulting report will under-count. Check `index=_internal sourcetype=splunkd log_level=WARN subsearch` for truncation warnings. For inner searches expected to return >10000 rows, rewrite as `| stats` with a larger base search and post-filter, or use `| tstats` if a CIM datamodel covers the data.

**Schedule configuration** (`savedsearches.conf`):
```ini
[UC-22.42.1: Unauthorized production change — no approved CR matches the ]
cron_schedule = */15 * * * *
dispatch.earliest_time = -1h
dispatch.latest_time = now
actions = summary_index
action.summary_index._name = summary_compliance
action.summary_index.marker = uc=22.42.1,reg=SOC_2
```

## Step 3 — Validate deployment

**(a) Field extraction check:** Run the following to confirm the TA is extracting every field the detection relies on:

```spl
index=change sourcetype=prod:change earliest=-1h
| fieldsummary
| where count=0
| table field
```

Any row returned is a field the TA claims to extract but isn't appearing in recent data — it's either a rename upstream, a TA version mismatch, or a data-format change.

**(b) Required-field presence:** This detection specifically needs: `_time`, `applied_by`, `change_id`, `system`, `system_owner`. Run:

```spl
index=change sourcetype=prod:change earliest=-1h
| head 100
| table _time, applied_by, change_id, system, system_owner
```

Every row should have non-null values for the fields the detection's `where` clause filters on. Nulls cause silent drops in the result set.

**(e) Negative test:** Generate a synthetic 'should not alert' event using the Splunk Sample Generator or a crafted `| makeresults` event and confirm the detection does NOT fire on it. A detection that fires on everything is equivalent to a detection that fires on nothing.

## Step 4 — Operationalize (dashboard and reporting)

Create a companion dashboard titled `UC-22.42.1: SOC 2 evidence` with the following panel layout:

**Panel 1 — Stacked Bar Approved Vs Unauthorised By System (the key fields):** Configure according to standard Splunk visualization patterns for this panel type.

**Panel 2 — Table Of Findings (the key fields):** Configure according to standard Splunk visualization patterns for this panel type.

**Panel 3 — Single-Value '% Authorised Changes' (the key fields):** Configure according to standard Splunk visualization patterns for this panel type.


Time picker defaults: set to last 30 days with named presets for 24h, 7d, 30d, 90d, and 1y — the auditor needs all five windows without retyping. Enable scheduled PDF export for the full dashboard on the regulation's reporting cadence.

## Step 5 — Troubleshooting and regulation-specific caveats

**No results when violations exist:** The most likely cause is subsearch truncation (see Step 2). Inspect `index=_internal sourcetype=splunkd component=searchprocessor log_level=WARN subsearch` for messages like 'Output result count exceeded 50000.'

**Search times out:** Check the search job inspector (`Activity > Jobs > <this UC's last run> > Inspect`). The phase consuming the time (fetch, command, summary) indicates whether the issue is data-layer (too wide a window, unaccelerated datamodel), command-layer (expensive `transaction` or `eventstats`), or post-aggregation.


**SOC 2-specific failure modes:**

• Trust Services Criteria scope: SOC 2 covers five TSCs (Security, Availability, Processing Integrity, Confidentiality, Privacy). Every UC must specify which TSCs it evidences; 'SOC 2' alone is ambiguous. Most organisations scope to Security + Availability — don't assume all five.

• Type 1 vs Type 2: Type 1 is point-in-time; Type 2 is 6-12 months of evidence. Detections supporting Type 2 must produce date-stamped evidence continuously, not just at snapshots — gaps in evidence are findings.

• CSOC (Control Owner / Service Organisation Control) assertions: SOC 2 depends on management's written assertion about the control. Detection output must be reconcilable to the assertion wording; a mismatch between 'what the control says' and 'what the detection measures' invalidates the evidence.

• Carve-out vs inclusive method: if subservice organisations are carved out, the UC must not cover them (they're not in scope). If inclusive, the UC must cover them even if owned by a third party — clarify in the UC metadata.
grandmaExplanation: We help you see when configs drift from what you baselined, so only approved change moves through.
